data.title: Treasury Yields on Path for Weekly Increase — Market Talk
data.description:
0842 ET - Treasury yields rise, on path for a weekly increase, as Trump picks economic adviser Stephen Miran for the Fed vacancy and markets wait for potential signs of tariff-driven inflation next week. July CPI is due Tuesday and some pundits forecast an acceleration from June's 0.3% pace. Treasury auctions this week stoked concerns about demand for long-term U.S. government debt. Markets are mostly pricing three interest rate cut by the Fed this year, according to CME. The 10-year is at 4.259% and the two-year at 3.742%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0838 ET - Employment in Canada fell for the second month this year in July, taking back a large chunk of the unexpectedly strong hiring the month before. The economy lost 40,800 jobs for the month following June's 83,100 gain, with the drop focused entirely on full-time positions. The weakness was felt most among youth in Canada, where unemployment rose to the highest since September 2010 outside the peak pandemic years. Still, with the labor force shrinking in July the overall unemployment rate was steady at 6.9% as the number of people looking for work or on temporary lay off was little changed. That contrasts with economists' consensus view the unemployment rate would tick up to 7% for the month.(robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0817 ET - The Swiss franc's reaction to the U.S. imposing 39% tariffs on Swiss goods has been relatively muted, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in a note. "This likely reflects market confidence that a tariff-reducing agreement will eventually be reached." The franc is also not particularly sensitive to domestic growth developments, which remain solid aside from the tariff news, they say. Hopes for lower tariffs along with July's higher-than-expected inflation data and the Swiss National Bank signalling a high hurdle to take rates below the current 0% level mean further rate cuts are unlikely at this stage, they say. The euro trades flat at 0.9405 francs and UBS expects it remain stable in a 0.93-0.94 range until next June. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0718 ET - Germany will likely have to revise down the already weak second-quarter GDP contraction of 0.1% due to industrial data that showed production slumped 1.9% on month in June, Berenberg's Holger Schmieding says. Whereas data had so far indicated that the slippage in 2Q was no more than a minor setback after two quarters of growth, new figures show a more pronounced reversal of fortunes, the analyst says in a note. However, slightly more upbeat surveys for July prompt a small lift to Berenberg's 3Q GDP forecasts from flat to 0.1%. Retail sales grew for a fifth quarter in a row in 2Q, suggesting German consumers are opening their wallets more, and companies also seem to be gearing up to invest more, he adds. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0712 ET - U.S. tariffs are massively damaging for the Swiss textile industry and caught everyone off guard, Swiss Textiles association says. The Trump administration implemented tariffs on goods imported from Switzerland of 39%. The country didn't expect this, as experts assumed a worst-case scenario of 31%, the organization says. The duties worsen the position of Swiss groups in relation to competitors from other countries, the trade association says. Last year, the Swiss textile and clothing industry exported goods worth 64 million Swiss francs to the U.S., putting the country in fifth place among the most important markets for the sector. "Swiss Textiles is calling for a continuation of the dialogue with the U.S. and for good relations with other partners such as the EU to be secured," it adds. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0705 ET - Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller would be the more dollar-supportive choice to become the central bank's next chief compared with some other potential candidates, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. Waller is emerging as a favorite to replace Fed Chair Jerome Powell, Bloomberg reported. "This should be viewed more positively by the financial markets than say Kevin Hassett who is deemed as very close to President Trump and with less experience." Trump has said Hassett, the director of the National Economic Council, is a frontrunner for the role along with former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh. The DXY dollar index falls 0.1% to 98.319. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0701 ET - Non-U.S. corporate debt remains attractive as an asset class, MFS Investment Management's Owen Murfin says in a note. It carries less risk than the U.S. credit, acting as a possible hedge against rising recession risk, he says. The global credit also provides diversification from increasing fiscal risks in some developed countries like the U.S., U.K., and Japan. The continuing monetary easing by central banks is also likely to boost credit performance, Murfin says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0640 ET - The good performance seen in euro-denominated credit is likely to continue as the eurozone economy is expected to recover, LBBW's Michael Koehler and Benedikt Horwedel say in a note. Euro credit spreads tightened this week on prospects of improved economic performance in the eurozone as governments raise infrastructure spending, the analysts say. "We continue to believe that the economy will stabilize and remain positive about credits." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0620 ET - The Canadian dollar could fall if upcoming data on the country's labor market are weak, Monex Europe analysts say in a note. A soft employment print would "crack open the door" for the Bank of Canada to resume cutting interest rates in the coming months, they say. This could push the U.S. dollar up to 1.38 Canadian dollars, they say. The U.S. dollar last trades down 0.1% at 1.3726 Canadian dollars. The data are due at 1230 GMT. Economists in a WSJ survey expect the data to show Canada added 10,000 jobs in July, marking a slowdown from 83,100 in June. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0616 ET - The European Central Bank will likely hold off from cutting rates in September as inflation settles at the bank's target, says Oxford Economics' Angel Talavera. Inflation held steady at 2.0% in July, figures showed last week. That will likely keep the ECB's governing council from moving again to lower borrowing costs, having last month held the deposit rate at 2.0%, Talavera says. "Although we still see inflation easing over the next few months, we don't think the progress on inflation will be enough for the council to agree to another cut by then," he writes in a note to investors. The ECB may later in the year cut rates if inflation shows signs of easing, but equally the central bank may decide that to leave them at current levels, Talavera says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0605 ET - Rabobank has a strong capital structure which lowers the possibility of it refinancing the Additional Tier 1 bonds callable in December 2025, ING's Suvi Kosonen says in a note. Additional tier 1 bonds (AT1 bonds) are a type of risky debt issued by banks to meet regulatory capital requirements. The bank targets a common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio of 14% but is likely to operate at a higher level than this in the medium term, Kosonen says. "We expect the bank to call its AT1 in December 2025 without refinancing," she says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0603 ET - The U.K. economy is likely to have slowed in the three months through June, Investec economist Philip Shaw writes in a note to clients. Figures for Britain's GDP will be set out next week and are likely to show a deceleration to growth of 0.2% for the quarter, down from the strong 0.7% growth booked in the first quarter of the year, according to Investec's estimates. That would give some vindication to the Bank of England's decision this week to lower interest rates by a quarter-point to 4.00%, Shaw says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: Fortrea Holdings Is Maintained at In-Line by Evercore ISI Group
FIRST CITIZENS BANCSHARES INC /DE/ SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
First Citizens BancShares Inc., a prominent financial institution operating predominantly in the Southeast, Mid-Atlantic, Midwest, and Western United States, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance and key business activities during the period.

Financial Highlights

For the three months ended June 30, 2025, First Citizens BancShares Inc. reported the following key financial metrics:

  • Total Interest Income: $2,945 million, reflecting a decrease from $3,130 million in the same period of 2024, primarily due to lower interest and fees on loans.
  • Net Interest Income: $1,695 million, down from $1,821 million in the same period of 2024, indicating a reduction in interest income relative to interest expense.
  • Net Income: $575 million, compared to $707 million in the same period of 2024, highlighting a decrease in profitability.
  • Net Income Available to Common Stockholders: $561 million, down from $691 million in the same period of 2024, reflecting the impact of preferred stock dividends.
  • Basic Earnings Per Common Share: $42.36, compared to $47.54 in the same period of 2024, indicating a decrease in earnings per share.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Common Share: $42.36, consistent with the basic earnings per share, compared to $47.54 in the same period of 2024.
  • Total Noninterest Income: $678 million, up from $639 million in the same period of 2024, driven by increases in rental income on operating lease equipment and lending-related fees.
  • Total Noninterest Expense: $1,500 million, up from $1,386 million in the same period of 2024, reflecting higher personnel costs and marketing expenses.
  • Income Before Income Taxes: $758 million, compared to $979 million in the same period of 2024, showing a decline in pre-tax income.
  • Income Tax Expense: $183 million, down from $272 million in the same period of 2024, corresponding to the decrease in pre-tax income.

Business Highlights

First Citizens BancShares Inc. also reported several key business activities and operational changes during the second quarter of 2025:

  • Segment Reporting Updates: During the first quarter of 2025, the company updated its segment reporting by transferring certain components from the Silicon Valley Bank (SVB) Commercial and General Bank segments to the Commercial Bank segment. This included modifying the segment expense allocation methodology.
  • Loan Class Changes: In the second quarter of 2025, the loan classes previously reported in the SVB portfolio were recast to the Commercial portfolio. This included combining investor dependent–early stage and growth stage into a single class and merging cash flow dependent and innovation C&I with the commercial and industrial loan class.
  • Geographical Operations: The company operates a network of branches and offices predominantly located in the Southeast, Mid-Atlantic, Midwest, and Western United States, providing various commercial and consumer banking services.
  • Business Combinations: On April 7, 2025, the company terminated the Shared-Loss Agreement with the FDIC related to the acquisition of Silicon Valley Bridge Bank, N.A., which had no impact on the consolidated balance sheets or statements of income.
  • Lease Operations: The company leases equipment to commercial end-users under operating and finance lease arrangements, with a significant portion of operating lease equipment being long-lived rail equipment.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates continued integration of the SVB acquisition and expects the changes in segment reporting and loan class adjustments to enhance operational efficiency and financial reporting clarity.

SEC Filing: FIRST CITIZENS BANCSHARES INC /DE/ [ FCNCA ] - 10-Q - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: Calumet Reports Second Quarter 2025 Results
data.description:
Calumet, Inc., a manufacturer and marketer of specialty branded products and renewable fuels, has released its financial results for the second quarter of 2025. The company reported a net loss, but also highlighted significant operational achievements and strategic initiatives.

Financial Highlights

For the second quarter of 2025, Calumet reported a net loss of $147.9 million, translating to a basic loss per common share of $1.70. This is a substantial increase from the net loss of $39.1 million, or $0.48 per share, reported in the same period last year. The company's revenue for the quarter was $1.03 billion, down from $1.13 billion in the second quarter of 2024.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter was $55.1 million, a decrease from $74.8 million in the same period last year. However, when including tax attributes, Adjusted EBITDA was $76.5 million, slightly up from $74.8 million in the previous year.

Business and Operational Highlights

Calumet's Specialty Products and Solutions (SPS) segment reported an Adjusted EBITDA of $66.8 million, down from $72.7 million in the same quarter last year. The Performance Brands (PB) segment reported an Adjusted EBITDA of $13.5 million, slightly down from $14.1 million in the second quarter of 2024. The Montana/Renewables (MR) segment showed significant improvement with an Adjusted EBITDA with Tax Attributes of $16.3 million, up from $8.7 million in the prior year period.

The company also highlighted its cost reduction initiatives, which have delivered $42 million in year-over-year operating cost savings through the first half of 2025. Additionally, Montana Renewables is on track to achieve 120–150 million gallons of annualized SAF production by the second quarter of 2026.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Calumet has been actively working on enhancing its capital structure, including calling $230 million of 2026 Senior Notes over the past four months. The company also completed a planned, month-long turnaround at its Shreveport facility, which was executed successfully.

Management's Perspective

CEO Todd Borgmann commented on the results, stating, "Our second quarter results reflect continued strength in our Specialties business, record operational performance at Montana Renewables, and meaningful cost reductions across the portfolio." He also emphasized the company's progress on strategic objectives, including the supportive regulatory environment for renewables and the ongoing MaxSAF™ expansion.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead, Calumet expects to continue its focus on cost reductions and operational efficiencies. The company is optimistic about the regulatory environment for renewables and anticipates achieving its SAF production targets by the second quarter of 2026. Additionally, Calumet aims to further improve its cash flow profile through disciplined operations and consistent commercial execution.

SEC Filing: Calumet, Inc. /DE [ CLMT ] - 8-K - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: Raymond James lifts Canadian Natural Resources' PT on higher production, improved unit costs
data.description:
** Raymond James raises PT on oil and gas producer Canadian Natural Resources to C$54 from C$52

** New PT represents upside of 25.2% to stock's last close

** "Production was in-line while cash flow from operations beat consensus and operating expenses were also better than expected," Raymond James says

** Brokerage views CNQ as one of higher quality upsteam names in Canada, with increasingly attractive valuation to match

** "We still view the balance sheet as rock solid given CNQ has one of the lowest sustaining free cash flow breakevens in Canada," Raymond James says

** Adds stock has lagged peer group YTD, creating compelling buying opportunity

** 14 out of 20 brokerages rate stock "buy" or higher, 6 "hold"; median PT is C$52 - data compiled by LSEG

** As of last close, CNQ stock down 5.8% YTD

data.title: Amlak Finance and DEWA Lead Dubai’s Recovery; Abu Dhabi edges lower
data.description:
Key points:
  • Dubai index gains on optimism over U.S. interest rate cuts
  • Amlak Finance and DEWA boost Dubai's market performance
  • Abu Dhabi index declines due to Dana Gas and E& losses

The Dubai index ended Friday on a positive note, led by investor optimism that U.S. interest rates could decline further this year, while Abu Dhabi headed in the opposite direction to finish lower.

U.S. President Donald Trump said on Thursday he would nominate Council of Economic Advisers Chairman Stephen Miran to fill a vacant seat at the Fed for a few months, while the White House seeks a permanent addition to the central bank's governing board and continues its search for a new chair.

Miran holds similar views to Trump, who has berated Chair Jerome Powell for being "too late" in cutting interest rates, even though growth is holding up and inflation is ticking higher.

The Fed's decisions impact monetary policy in the Gulf, where most currencies, including the UAE dirham, are pegged to the U.S. dollar.

Dubai's main index rebounded on Friday, recovering from two sessions of losses with the index settling 0.3% higher.

Gains were underpinned by a 5.3% surge in Amlak Finance and a 0.6% increase in Emirates Central Cooling Systems Corporation .

State-run utility giant Dubai Electricity And Water Authority rose 0.4% after second-quarter net profit jumped more than 25% to 2.26 billion dirhams ($615.3 million).

By contrast Abu Dhabi's benchmark index slipped 0.1%, weighed down by a 1.2% decline in Sharjah-based Dana Gas and a 2% decline in UAE's largest telecom provider Emirates Telecommunication Group (better known as E&) .

Dana Gas reported a 12% decrease in its second-quarter net profit to AED 112 million ($30.5 million) on the back of reduced oil production volumes.

Losses in the index were offset by a 0.8% rise in hypermarket operator Lulu Retail Holdings and a 1.7% gain in data analytical firm Alpha Data ahead of their earnings next week.

Oil - a key contributor to Gulf economies - was stable at 0.69% to $66.89 a barrel by 1140 GMT.

ABU DHABI

<.FTFADGI> down 0.1% to 10,312

DUBAI

.DFMGI up 0.3% to 6,149

($1 = 3.6728 UAE dirham)

data.title: Veidekke: Building sports arena in Bergen
data.description:
Veidekke has been commissioned to build the multi-purpose facility Bjarg Arenain Fana in Bergen. The assignment for the Bjarg Sports Club is a turnkeycontract worth NOK 116 million excl. VAT.

"We are very grateful to have won the competition to build the arena, whichwillmean so much to the quality of life of so many people, not least children andyoung people in the district. We are very grateful for the assignment and lookforward to seeing the facility in full operation in just over a year," saysdistrict manager Thor Småbrekke in Veidekke Bygg Bergen.

Bjarg Arena will be approximately 5,000 m2 and will be universally designed.Itwill be adapted for, among other things, handball, basketball, football,gymnastics, martial arts, athletics, badminton, dance, fitness, yoga andstrength training. When completed, the sports facility will include a handballhall with 230 spectator seats, a basketball hall, an activity hall, a gym,running tracks in the basement and a football room in addition to lockerrooms,club rooms and a cafeteria.

Construction work will begin immediately with completion planned around thestart of school in the fall of 2026.

The contract is included in Veidekke's order book for Q3 2025.

For further information, please contact:

District Manager Thor Småbrekke, Veidekke Bygg Bergen, tel. +47 93 43 06 60,thor.smaabrekke@veidekke.no (are.westbye@veidekke.no)

Communications Manager Helge Dieset, Veidekke, tel. +47 90 55 33 22,helge.dieset@veidekke.no

Veidekke's press photos (https://veidekke.fotoware.cloud/fotoweb/archives/5028-Pressebilder-Veidekke/)Subscribe to notices fromVeidekke (http://veidekke.com/no/borsmeldinger/abonner-pa-meldinger/)Veidekke (http://www.veidekke.com/) is one of Scandinavia's largestcontractors.In addition to undertaking all types of building and civil engineeringassignments, the group also maintains roads and produces asphalt andaggregates.Veidekke emphasises stakeholder involvement and local experience. data.title: Canada Employment Unexpectedly Shrinks
data.description:
Canada’s employment fell by 40,800 in July 2025, following a 83,100 increase in June and missing market forecasts of a 13,500 rise.

The drop was mainly due to the loss of 51,000 full-time positions, which outweighed a gain of 10,300 part-time jobs.

Employment was down across several industries, led by information, culture and recreation (-29,000; -3.3%) and construction (-22,000; -1.3%).

data.title: Amazon Offers $1B in Cloud Savings to U.S. Government
data.description:
Amazon Web Services (AMZN, Financials) has committed to give U.S. government agencies up to $1 billion in savings through 2028 to help them modernize old systems and use AI and cloud capabilities.

The General Services Administration announced the partnership on Thursday. It gives credits for AWS services including training, upgrading infrastructure, and moving to the cloud. It aims to save money and speed up the digital transformation of federal agencies.

AWS already works with over 11,000 governments around the world. Matt Garman, the CEO, said that the pact was a big step toward making the government more flexible and tech-savvy.

The timing is right since there is more and more pressure on Washington to fix old infrastructure. The federal government spends more than $100 billion a year on IT, and a lot of it goes to antiquated, slow systems.

It's not only about getting contracts for Amazon; it's also about staying at the core of how America constructs its digital future.

data.title: Under Armour's stock dives on a weak outlook, with tariffs and a slowing economy to blame
data.description:
By James Rogers

Under Armour's current-quarter outlook for sales growth and profit fell below Wall Street's expectations, and the company again did not provide a full-year outlook

Under Armour reported fiscal first-quarter results before market open.

Shares of Under Armour Inc. tumbled in early Friday trading, after the maker of athletic gear gave a weaker-than-expected outlook for current-quarter sales and profit.

The company blamed its downbeat guidance on "ongoing uncertainty around trade policies and the broader macroeconomic environment," including potential demand losses and cost impacts from tariffs. Like last quarter, the company did not provide a full-year outlook.

The stock (UAA) sank 15.8% in premarket trading. That put it on track to suffer the biggest single-day postearnings drop since a 23.8% fall on May 6, 2022.

Revenue for the current fiscal second quarter is expected to decline 6% to 7% for the same period a year ago, while the FactSet consensus estimate calls for a 2.9% decline.

Under Armour's revenue outlook includes an anticipated low-double-digit percentage decrease in North America, the company said.

The company expects a second-quarter loss of 7 cents to 8 cents a share. On an adjusted basis, Under Armour expects earnings between 1 cent and 2 cents a share, well below the FactSet consensus estimate of 26 cents a share.

For the fiscal first quarter to June 30, revenue declined 4% to $1.134 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet were looking for revenue of $1.132 billion. The company's North American revenue fell 5% to $670 million, while international revenue declined 1% to $467 million.

The company reported a net loss of $2.6 million, or a loss of 1 cent a share, after a loss of $305.4 million, or a loss of 70 cents a share, in the prior year's quarter.

On an adjusted basis, Under Armour earned 2 cents a share, compared with 1 cent a share in the prior year's quarter. Analysts surveyed by FactSet were looking for adjusted earnings of 3 cents a share.

Under Armour's recently announced restructuring plan resulted in $71 million in restructuring and impairment charges during the quarter, the company said, as well as $39 million in other related transformational expenses.

The company's cost of goods sold was $587.6 million, or 51.8% of net revenue, in line with the FactSet consensus estimate of $588 million. Cost of goods sold refers to the direct costs incurred in order to sell a product or service and is an important metric in calculating a company's profitability.

Under Armour's stock has dropped 19.8% in 2025 through Thursday, while rival Nike Inc. shares (NKE) have slipped 1.7% and the S&P 500 index SPX has advanced 7.8%.

-James Rogers

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Shift in Consumer Trends Drives Growth for Arctic Blue Beverages’ Emerging Portfolio Stars
data.description:
Nightwish Gin, White Label concepts, and innovative Long Drinks fuel sales momentum in key markets.

Sales of Arctic Blue Beverages’ newest offerings are surging as consumers embrace customization, fandom-driven products, and convenient ready-to-drink (RTD) options.

Arctic Blue Beverages is witnessing a significant shift in consumer demand, with innovative products taking center stage in its portfolio. Today’s customers seek personalization, express identity through purchasing choices, and increasingly opt for premium yet convenient beverage solutions.

Nightwish Gin Becomes a Fan-Favorite

The standout performer is Nightwish Gin, created in collaboration with the iconic Finnish symphonic metal band Nightwish. This unique product has quickly outpaced other bestsellers in Arctic Blue Beverages’ lineup, driven by strong e-commerce sales and rising demand in the European market. Joint marketing campaigns with the band and visibility at selected music events have amplified interest, making Nightwish Gin a hit among core fan communities.

White Label Gins Offer Personalization for Brands and Individuals

Another exciting innovation is Arctic Blue Distillery’s customizable White Label Gin, available for both businesses and private customers. Based on the signature Coastal Dry Gin recipe, buyers can choose from partially customized or fully personalized label designs, offering a way to integrate brand identity or personal flair into a premium gin experience.

This White Label concept is gaining traction in the HoReCa segment, where hospitality businesses seek to elevate their guest experience with branded gin offerings. It’s also proving popular for corporate gifts and special occasions, providing a distinctive and memorable option.

Long Drinks Reinvent Convenience with Bold New Flavors

Meanwhile, the Long Drink category continues its upward trajectory as consumers seek premium, convenient choices. Arctic Blue’s innovative flavors, Laku (liquorice) and Rose, have quickly won fans in Finland through Kesko retail stores and are now reaching international customers via the company’s e-commerce platform. These RTDs are also available through selected HoReCa channels.

Looking Ahead: HoReCa, E-Commerce & U.S. Expansion

Under new leadership, Arctic Blue Beverages is doubling down on serving HoReCa customers in Finland with personalized support, enhancing the Arctic Blue Distillery Showroom experience, expanding e-commerce with robust marketing, and working on bringing Long Drinks to the U.S. market. The strong sales performance of these products reflects the success of this refreshed strategic focus.

“The popularity of these products underscores our commitment to offering customers the choices they want, without compromising our core values and sustainable growth,” says Petri Hirvonen, CEO of Arctic Blue Beverages.

Arctic Blue Beverages will continue to prioritize these key offerings, with expectations for sustained growth across the portfolio.

For more information please contact

Petri Hirvonen, CEO

Phone: +358 40 5710 834

Email: petri.hirvonen@arcticbluebeverages.com

Pauliina Marjanen, Marketing Manager

Phone: +358 50 336 5986

Email: pauliina.marjanen@arcticbluebeverages.com

data.title: First Seacoast Bancorp, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
First Seacoast Bancorp, Inc., operating through its wholly-owned subsidiary First Seacoast Bank, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report provides a detailed overview of the company's financial performance and operational highlights, reflecting the challenges and achievements faced during the period.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Interest and Dividend Income: $13.155 million, reflecting an increase from $12.348 million in the same period last year, driven by higher interest and fees on loans.
  • Net Interest and Dividend Income: $6.607 million, up from $5.915 million, indicating improved interest income despite rising interest expenses.
  • Net (Loss) Income: $(1.148) million, compared to a net income of $0.851 million in the prior year, primarily due to increased non-interest expenses and a decrease in non-interest income.
  • (Loss) Income Per Share - Basic: $(0.27), down from $0.18, reflecting the net loss incurred during the period.
  • (Loss) Income Per Share - Diluted: $(0.27), consistent with basic earnings per share, as the effect of potentially dilutive shares was not included due to the net loss.

Business Highlights

  • Corporate Structure: First Seacoast Bancorp, Inc. operates through its wholly-owned subsidiary, First Seacoast Bank, which offers a full range of banking and investment management services. The Bank focuses on four core services: residential lending, commercial banking, personal banking, and wealth management.
  • Geographical Performance: The Bank's lending activities are primarily conducted in and around Dover, New Hampshire, and in the areas surrounding its branches. This regional focus influences the Bank's ability to attract deposits and issue loans.
  • Loan Portfolio Composition: The Bank's loan portfolio includes commercial real estate loans, multifamily loans, commercial and industrial loans, acquisition, development, and land loans, 1-4 family residential loans, home equity lines of credit, and consumer loans. Most loans are collateralized by real estate.
  • Loan Servicing: The Bank services loans for others, with an unpaid principal balance of $30.1 million as of June 30, 2025. These loans are primarily fixed-rate residential mortgages originated by the Bank and sold to third parties on a non-recourse basis.
  • Future Outlook: The Bank continues to focus on its core services and regional market, with an emphasis on maintaining strong credit quality and managing interest rate risk. The Bank's management regularly assesses performance and allocates resources based on net income or loss.

SEC Filing: First Seacoast Bancorp, Inc. [ FSEA ] - 10-Q - Aug. 08, 2025

data.title: Burke & Herbert Financial Services Corp. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Burke & Herbert Financial Services Corp., a prominent financial services company operating primarily in northern Virginia and West Virginia, has released its latest Form 10-Q report. The report highlights significant improvements in the company's financial performance, driven by higher interest rates and loan growth, as well as a detailed overview of its business operations and future outlook.

Financial Highlights

Total Interest Income: $111.9 million, reflecting a significant increase from the previous year due to higher interest rates and loan growth.

Net Interest Income: $74.2 million, driven by increased interest income from loans and securities.

Net Income: $29.9 million, a substantial improvement from a loss in the previous year, indicating strong financial recovery.

Net Income (Loss) Applicable to Common Shares: $29.7 million, highlighting the company's profitability after accounting for preferred stock dividends.

Basic Earnings (Loss) Per Common Share: $1.98, showing a positive turnaround from a loss per share in the previous year.

Diluted Earnings (Loss) Per Common Share: $1.97, consistent with the basic earnings per share, reflecting the company's improved financial performance.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company's loan portfolio is segmented into commercial real estate, owner-occupied commercial real estate, acquisition, construction & development, commercial & industrial, single family residential (1-4 units), and consumer non-real estate and other. Each segment carries distinct risks associated with market conditions, interest rates, and economic factors.

Geographical Performance: The primary market area includes northern Virginia and West Virginia, with over 77 branches and commercial loan offices across Delaware, Kentucky, Maryland, Virginia, and West Virginia. The company accepts business and consumer deposits from a diverse customer base in these regions.

Sales Units: The company reported a decrease in loans held-for-sale from $2,331,000 at the end of 2024 to $1,511,000 as of June 30, 2025, indicating a reduction in the volume of loans available for sale.

Future Outlook: The company anticipates continued regulatory compliance and capital adequacy, with a focus on maintaining a well-capitalized status under regulatory frameworks. The management expects to recover the entire amortized cost basis of securities, indicating a stable outlook for the investment portfolio.

SEC Filing: Burke & Herbert Financial Services Corp. [ BHRB ] - 10-Q - Aug. 08, 2025
